issue.errorLine1: <string name="help_tip_security_sandbox_p1_desc">Sandboxing protects the app by processing untrusted files — such as HEIF, AVIF, and JXL images — in isolated system processes. If a malicious file exploits a decoder vulnerability, the damage is contained in the sandbox and cannot reach the main app or your data.</string>

issue.errorLine1: <string name="help_tip_security_sandbox_p2_desc">You can control how aggressively files are sandboxed. Metadata isolation determines whether EXIF parsing happens in a shared process, per-file process, or a hybrid of both. Sandboxed image decoding routes HEIF/AVIF/JXL through an isolated decoder service using shared memory.</string>

issue.errorLine1:
<string name="security_metadata_isolation_summary">Controls how metadata parsing is isolated between files. All modes run in a separate process with no permissions — the difference is how often that process is recycled.

Shared process — All files are parsed through a single long-lived isolated process. Fastest option with minimal overhead for library scanning. If a malicious file crashes the parser, the process is restarted automatically. Recommended for most users.

Hybrid — Files you open in the viewer get a fresh isolated process; background indexing reuses a shared one. Actively viewed files are fully isolated from each other, while batch operations stay fast. Recommended if you occasionally receive files from untrusted sources.

Per-file — Every single file gets its own isolated process, destroyed immediately after parsing. Strongest isolation with zero cross-contamination, but significantly slower for large libraries and uses more battery during scanning. Only recommended if you regularly handle untrusted files.</string>

If the decoder encounters a malformed file, it cannot affect the main app.\n\nThis adds a small overhead (~5–15%) to initial image loads but has no effect on cached thumbnails.</string>

issue.errorLine1:
<string name="security_private_folder_description">Designate a folder on your device that is only accessible through this app. Its contents are completely hidden from the normal timeline, albums, and search results.

How it works — The folder is accessed through the Storage Access Framework (SAF), which means its contents are not indexed by the system media scanner and will not appear in other gallery apps or file managers that rely on MediaStore. When you pick a folder, a .nomedia file is automatically created inside it to prevent Android from scanning its contents. This ensures photos and videos in the folder stay hidden from all other apps.

Where you can find it — Once configured, a "Private folder" entry appears in your album list. Opening it requires authentication if you have app lock enabled.

What you can do — Browse, view, and delete photos and videos inside the private folder. Files are displayed in a dedicated viewer with the same features as the main gallery (zoom, swipe, metadata).

Choosing a folder — You can pick any folder your device allows, including internal storage, SD card, or a USB drive. The app remembers your choice across restarts. You can change or remove the folder at any time without affecting the files themselves.

Important — Do not delete the .nomedia file inside the private folder. If removed, Android will scan the folder and its contents may appear in other gallery apps. The app will recreate it automatically the next time you open the private folder settings.</string>

Think of it as your gallery\'s control center.</string>
